conceptTitle: Arrays, Stacks, Queues and Linked Lists
reference: GATE CS — Programming and Data Structures / Linear Data Structures

map.condition: Expected time for successful search in chained hash table (load factor \alpha)
Infix-to-postfix conversion using stack (operator precedence clash)
Circular queue: number of slots occupied

formulas.name: 1-D Array — Element Address
formulas.plain: addr(A[i]) = B + (i - L) * s
formulas.whenToUse: Finding the memory address of A[i] given base address B, lower bound L, and element size s.

terms.means: size of each element in bytes / words

formulas.trap: Forgetting to subtract L when arrays are not 0-based (e.g. A[1..n]).

formulas.name: 2-D Array — Row-Major Address
formulas.plain: addr(A[i][j]) = B + [(i - R1) * C + (j - C1)] * s
formulas.whenToUse: Row-major (C-style) storage; C = number of columns in the declared range.

terms.means: lower bounds of row and column indices

formulas.trap: Using the wrong major order — swap rows and columns for column-major (Fortran/Pascal).

formulas.name: Lower Triangular Matrix — Packed Row-Major Index (0-based)
formulas.plain: addr(A[i][j]) = B + (i*(i+1)/2 + j) * s,  for j <= i
formulas.whenToUse: Compact (packed) storage of an n×n lower triangular matrix (elements with j > i are zero and not stored).

terms.means: total elements in rows 0 through i-1

formulas.trap: Applying this formula to upper triangular matrices without transposing; also forgetting that the formula changes for 1-based indexing.

formulas.name: Two-Pointer Pair-Sum in Sorted Array
formulas.latex: \text{while } l < r:\; \text{if } A[l]+A[r]=M \Rightarrow \text{found};\; A[l]+A[r]<M \Rightarrow l{+}{+};\; \text{else } r{-}{-}
formulas.plain: l=1, r=n; while l<r: if A[l]+A[r]==M found; elif sum<M: l++; else r--
formulas.whenToUse: Finding indices i, j with A[i]+A[j] = M in a sorted array in O(n) time.

terms.means: left and right pointers starting at ends of the array

formulas.trap: Forgetting that the array must be sorted; a naive O(n^2) search is wrong for O(n) requirement.

formulas.name: Division Hash Function
formulas.plain: h(k) = k mod m
formulas.whenToUse: Basic hash function mapping key k to one of m buckets/slots.

terms.means: key value (or ordinal representation of key)

formulas.trap: Choosing m as a power of 2 makes the hash depend only on low-order bits; prefer m prime.

formulas.name: Load Factor
formulas.plain: alpha = n / m
formulas.whenToUse: Measuring how full a hash table is; drives expected search/insert time analysis.
formulas.trap: For open addressing alpha must stay < 1; for chaining alpha can exceed 1.

formulas.name: Expected Search Time — Chaining (Uniform Hashing)
formulas.plain: Successful: Theta(1 + alpha/2),  Unsuccessful: Theta(1 + alpha)
formulas.whenToUse: Expected number of comparisons for search in a chained hash table under simple uniform hashing.
formulas.trap: GATE often asks about worst-case (O(n) for chaining) vs expected-case — do not confuse them.

formulas.name: Linear Probing — Probe Sequence
formulas.latex: h(k, i) = (h'(k) + i) \bmod m, \quad i = 0, 1, \ldots, m-1
formulas.plain: h(k, i) = (h'(k) + i) mod m
formulas.whenToUse: Open-addressing collision resolution where the next slot is probed sequentially.
formulas.trap: Linear probing causes primary clustering; double hashing avoids this.

formulas.name: Chaining vs Open Addressing — Key Properties
formulas.latex: \text{Chaining: deletion easy, } \alpha > 1 \text{ allowed} \quad|\quad \text{Open addr: no extra memory, } \alpha < 1 \text{ required}
formulas.plain: Chaining: easy deletion, alpha>1 ok | Open addressing: no pointers, alpha<1 required
formulas.whenToUse: Deciding which collision-resolution strategy to use or answering comparison questions.
formulas.trap: Chaining does NOT have better worst-case search time than open addressing in general (both are O(n) worst case).

formulas.name: Infix-to-Postfix Algorithm (Shunting Yard)
formulas.plain: While prec(stack.top) >= prec(incoming_op): pop and output; then push incoming_op
formulas.whenToUse: Converting infix arithmetic expressions to postfix (RPN) using a single operator stack.
formulas.trap: Exponentiation ^ is right-associative: pop only if prec(top) > prec(^), not >=.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Infix Prefix

formulas.name: Standard Operator Precedence
formulas.plain: ^ (highest) > { *, / } > { +, - } (lowest)
formulas.whenToUse: Determining which operator to pop when two operators of different precedence meet during expression conversion.
formulas.trap: Right-associativity of ^ means a^b^c = a^(b^c); pop ^ only when incoming precedence is strictly lower.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Infix Prefix

formulas.name: Postfix Expression Evaluation
formulas.latex: \text{Scan left-to-right: push operands; on operator } \otimes \text{: pop } b, a; \text{ push } (a \otimes b)
formulas.plain: Scan L-to-R: push numbers; on operator: pop b then a, push (a op b)
formulas.whenToUse: Evaluating a postfix (RPN) expression using an operand stack.

terms.means: operands; a is popped second (was pushed first)

formulas.trap: Order matters for non-commutative operators: for a-b or a/b, a is the second pop, not the first.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Infix Prefix

formulas.name: Stack ADT — Operations
formulas.plain: push(x), pop() -> O(1); Last-In First-Out (LIFO)
formulas.whenToUse: Any GATE question about stack complexity or LIFO property.
formulas.trap: A stack can be implemented with an array or linked list; neither changes the O(1) amortized push/pop.

formulas.name: Queue ADT — FIFO Property
formulas.plain: enqueue(x), dequeue() -> O(1); First-In First-Out (FIFO)
formulas.whenToUse: BFS, scheduling, printer spooling — anywhere FIFO ordering is required.
formulas.trap: Simple array queue wastes space on dequeue; use circular queue or dynamic array.

formulas.name: Circular Queue — Number of Elements
formulas.plain: count = (rear - front + SIZE) mod SIZE
formulas.whenToUse: Determining how many elements are in a circular queue given front and rear pointers.

terms.means: total allocated slots in the circular buffer
index of the last element (or next-to-insert, depending on convention)

formulas.trap: A circular queue of SIZE slots holds at most SIZE-1 elements (one slot kept empty to distinguish full from empty).

formulas.name: Circular Queue — Full & Empty Conditions
formulas.plain: Empty: front == rear;  Full: (rear+1) mod SIZE == front
formulas.whenToUse: Checking full/empty state in a circular queue implemented with one wasted slot.

terms.means: indices maintained by the queue; front points to first element

formulas.trap: If no wasted slot is used, a counter variable is needed to distinguish full from empty.

formulas.name: Double-Ended Queue (Deque) — Key Property
formulas.latex: \text{insert/delete at both front and rear in } O(1)
formulas.plain: insert/delete at both ends in O(1)
formulas.whenToUse: Sliding window maximum, palindrome checking, problems needing both-end access.
formulas.trap: A deque generalises both stack and queue but does not sort elements.

formulas.name: Singly Linked List — Insert/Delete at Head/Tail
formulas.latex: \text{Insert at head: } O(1); \quad \text{Delete head: } O(1); \quad \text{Delete tail: } O(n)
formulas.plain: Insert head: O(1); Delete head: O(1); Delete tail: O(n) for SLL
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing linked list operations; choosing between head/tail insertion.

terms.means: number of nodes in the list

formulas.trap: Deleting the last node in a singly linked list requires O(n) traversal (no back pointer).
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Linked List

formulas.name: Doubly Linked List — Insert/Delete
formulas.latex: \text{Insert/Delete at any given pointer: } O(1); \quad \text{Delete tail with tail pointer: } O(1)
formulas.plain: Insert/Delete with given node pointer: O(1); tail delete: O(1) with DLL
formulas.whenToUse: When backward traversal or O(1) deletion at any known position is needed.

terms.means: backward and forward pointers in each node

formulas.trap: DLL uses extra memory (one extra pointer per node); also needs careful pointer update (4 pointer changes for insert).
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Linked List

formulas.name: Reverse a Singly Linked List — O(n)
formulas.plain: prev=NULL; while curr!=NULL: next=curr.next; curr.next=prev; prev=curr; curr=next
formulas.whenToUse: Reversing a linked list in O(n) time with O(1) extra space (three-pointer technique).
formulas.trap: Must save curr.next BEFORE overwriting curr.next; otherwise the list is lost.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Linked List

formulas.name: Floyd's Cycle Detection (Linked List)
formulas.plain: slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next; cycle if slow == fast
formulas.whenToUse: Detecting a cycle in a linked list in O(n) time and O(1) space.

terms.means: pointer advancing one step at a time
pointer advancing two steps at a time

formulas.trap: If fast reaches NULL (or fast.next is NULL) before meeting slow, there is no cycle.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Linked List

formulas.name: Linked List — Search Complexity
formulas.plain: Search: O(n); no O(1) random access unlike arrays
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing linked lists to arrays for random-access vs dynamic-size trade-off questions.
formulas.trap: Binary search cannot be applied to a linked list (no O(1) index access), so sorted linked list search is still O(n).
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Linked List

formulas.name: Abstract Data Type (ADT) — Definition
formulas.plain: ADT = { hidden data representation } + { defined operations }; users can only use the defined operations
formulas.whenToUse: Answering conceptual GATE questions about what an ADT is vs a concrete data structure.

terms.means: Abstract Data Type — specifies behaviour (what), not implementation (how)

formulas.trap: An ADT is NOT the same as an abstract class in OOP; it is a mathematical specification.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Data Structures

formulas.name: Stack / Queue — Array vs Linked List Implementation Complexity
formulas.latex: \text{Array: } O(1) \text{ amortized (resizing);} \quad \text{Linked list: } O(1) \text{ worst-case per op}
formulas.plain: Array (dynamic): O(1) amortized; Linked list: O(1) worst-case per push/pop/enqueue/dequeue
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing implementations when GATE asks about time/space trade-offs.
formulas.trap: Array-based stack may overflow if fixed-size; linked-list-based uses extra pointer memory.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Data Structures

formulas.name: BFS Uses Queue; DFS Uses Stack
formulas.plain: BFS -> Queue (FIFO);  DFS -> Stack (LIFO) or recursion call stack
formulas.whenToUse: Graph traversal questions that ask which data structure is required.
formulas.trap: Iterative DFS explicitly uses a stack; recursive DFS uses the implicit call stack.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Data Structures
